turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Good morning, this is The Morning Commute. Friday, July tenth. I'm Sam.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: And I'm Alex. The throughline today is a reflation scare the market is choosing to buy. In three weeks the story flipped — the Iran ceasefire collapsed, China's factory prices hit a 4-year high, and the Fed's own dot plot swung toward a hike. Yields backed up, gold's sitting near 4,100, and yet stocks keep printing records on the AI-memory boom. Real things and compounding earnings, bid at the same time. Let's get into it.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Headlines. World desk first, and it's the one we hoped we wouldn't be doing — Iran.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The June ceasefire is dead. The Islamabad Memorandum — signed June seventeenth by Trump and Pezeshkian — held barely three weeks. On July seventh two tankers were struck in the Strait of Hormuz: the Qatari LNG carrier Al Rekayat, which took an engine-room fire and was evacuated, and the Saudi supertanker Wedyan. Then the IRGC hit a third.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the US response was not incremental.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Not remotely. Treasury reimposed the oil sanctions it had just lifted, CENTCOM struck more than 80 Iranian targets — air defense, coastal radar, 60-plus IRGC fast-boats — and Trump declared the truce over. By the eighth and ninth Iran was hitting US bases in Bahrain, Kuwait and Qatar. We're back in a hot conflict.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So why is Brent only at 76 and change? A year ago that headline is a 10-dollar gap up.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's the tell for the day. The tankers are still moving through Hormuz, and as long as the flow physically continues the market prices probability-times-severity, not the headline. Brent's about 76.5, WTI 72.2 — war premium rebuilding, but capped. The option value is in the tail: one closed strait and this re-rates violently. For now it's a slow bleed higher, not a shock.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: A premium, not a panic. Second world story — Ankara.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The NATO summit. Two things that matter for markets. Trump met Zelenskyy and said the US will license Ukraine to manufacture Patriot systems domestically — that's technology transfer, not just deliveries. And nine countries, Canada out front, stood up a global defence bank to finance allied rearmament.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: A dedicated balance sheet for the security-capex cycle. That's the institutional plumbing catching up to the theme.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Exactly. Rearmament's been a multi-year capex supercycle the market's under-positioned for — build a supranational lender specifically to fund it and you've made it structural. Money follows geopolitics, and it just told you where it's going for a decade.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Good. Let's do the tape. Market overview.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Thursday's close, and it's records. S&P 500 up 81 basis points to 7,543.64. Nasdaq Composite up a full 1.3 percent to 26,206.89. Dow up 27 basis points to 52,487.41. Russell 2000 up to 2,992.54. VIX pinned at 15.9.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the leadership is the whole point. This wasn't broad — it was semiconductors.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Memory, specifically. The semis ETF, SMH, up 2.5 percent. Micron up 4.5, SanDisk up 7.6. The HBM complex is the engine of this tape, jolted by the SK Hynix listing we'll get to. When people say the AI trade's defensive right now — look at the tape. It's the offense.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: But under the equity records, the bond market is telling a different story. Yields backed up.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's the reflation tell, and the crux of the day. The 10-year's at 4.54, up from 4.48 Monday. Two-year 4.16, 30-year above 5. Fed funds held at 3.5 to 3.75 a fourth straight meeting. June's disinflation trade — cheaper oil, lower yields, a Fed with cover to cut — has quietly reversed.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the minutes made it official. Walk me through the dots, because this is a genuine regime shift.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The June minutes dropped Wednesday and they're a different Fed. Nine of eighteen participants now see at least one hike before year-end. Eight, no change. Exactly one sees a cut. The dovish dissent that dominated the spring has vanished — a few even flagged a case for hiking in June.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: That's a 9-to-1 skew toward hiking versus cutting. Six weeks ago the debate was how many cuts.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: And it maps onto everything else — oil firming, China reflating, core services sticky. The wrinkle is Warsh himself: the new chair submitted no dot and ran a roughly 130-word statement. He's deliberately telling you nothing, which forces the market to trade the committee's distribution, not the chair's guidance. Opacity as a policy tool.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Which brings us to the loudest instrument in the room. Gold.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Gold around 4,116, silver near 60. That's off January's spike above 5,300, so not a fresh record — but structurally extraordinary, and bid for reasons that rhyme with the whole show. The PBoC bought a 19th straight month. A record 45 percent of official reserve managers say they intend to add. And the deficit-and-debasement trade is the backdrop — JPMorgan's still carrying a 5,000 target.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So gold and AI-semis, both bid. Frame the barbell, because that's the throughline in one picture.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Exactly. When the market smells reflation it doesn't sell — it rotates into what can't be printed and what compounds faster than inflation. Hard assets on one end, the AI-memory buildout on the other. Gold's the debasement hedge; the semis are the growth hedge. Same trade, two faces. What gets sold is the middle — long-duration nominal bonds — and that's your yields backing up.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Rounding out the board — dollar and oil.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Dollar firm. Euro at 1.143, near the year's low. Dollar-yen at 161.8, near the year's high — yen still weak despite the BOJ at 1 percent, because the rate differential is what matters. Dollar index around 100.6. Oil we covered — Brent 76.5, WTI 72.2.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: US business. Two, and the first is the biggest listing on the tape in years.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: SK Hynix went public on the Nasdaq Thursday, ticker SKHY. They priced 177.9 million ADRs at 149 dollars — a 3 percent premium to the Korean close — raising about 26.5 billion. That's one of the largest US listings ever by a foreign company, at a market cap around 1.1 trillion.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the book was ludicrous. Seven times oversubscribed?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: About seven times, Baillie Gifford and Coatue anchoring. And the reason is one number: SK Hynix holds roughly 58 percent of the global high-bandwidth-memory market, and ranks one or two in DRAM, HBM and NAND. US institutions paying up for the purest direct claim on the memory supercycle that exists.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So this isn't a de-risking market shy of AI. This is the market writing a 26-billion-dollar check to get more of it.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's the tell. The debate for a month was whether the AI trade was overextended. The order book for the single cleanest AI-memory name says the marginal institutional dollar disagrees — hard. It's why the whole semis complex traded the way it did into the print.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Deal two — and it's a real-economy one. Kroger.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Kroger's buying Giant Eagle for 1.65 billion — 1.25 in cash plus about 400 million of assumed liabilities. Giant Eagle's a family-owned Ohio and Pennsylvania grocer, roughly 9 billion in sales, 197 supermarkets and 11 pharmacies.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Kroger got burned trying to buy Albertsons. Why does this one clear when that one didn't?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Footprint. The overlap here is limited, so the antitrust math is far friendlier than the Albertsons overlap that sank the last attempt. They've signaled some divestitures to get it through. The National Grocers Association is pushing back for the independents, and it won't close before 2027 — but this is the achievable version of the deal Kroger wanted.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the signal underneath it.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Same one we keep hitting — when organic growth is scarce, boards buy it. Late-cycle consolidation, and the pipeline keeps filling. Two international to close the block.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The ECB. The July ninth meeting accounts defended June's hike to a 2.25 percent deposit rate as, quote, robust across scenarios, with staff seeing euro-area inflation averaging 3 percent this year.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So Europe is doing exactly what the Fed minutes just hinted at — tightening into the reflation, not easing out of a slowdown.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's the synchronization that matters. A year ago the central-bank story was a coordinated cutting cycle. Now the ECB's hiked, the BOJ's at 1, the Fed's dots point up, and even the Philippine central bank's on hold after hiking. The whole policy world has pivoted from supporting growth to guarding against inflation. A regime, not a data point.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Second story — France, and it's a political-risk one.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Marine Le Pen says she'll contest the 2027 presidency despite being handed a court-ordered electronic monitor this week for embezzlement. So the far-right standard-bearer runs from under a legal cloud.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the market expression is the sovereign spread.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The OAT-Bund spread is the fear gauge. French political risk plus an already-strained fiscal position is the cocktail that widens it — a slow-burn re-rating, not a gap. But with European rates rising for inflation reasons too, it's the story to keep on the radar. China to close the block.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: This is the cleanest read on the whole reflation thread, and it flips a narrative we've been running for months. China's producer prices rose 4.1 percent year-on-year in June — the strongest since July 2022, and a fourth straight monthly gain.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: For a country that was the poster child for exported deflation, that is a genuine turn. What's driving it?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Two things, and they're different in kind. One is cyclical — firmer global commodities feeding through, production materials up 5.5, mining prices up 16.5. The other is policy, and it's the interesting one: Beijing's anti-involution campaign — a deliberate crackdown on cut-throat, capacity-glut price wars that were manufacturing deflation.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So the state is engineering pricing power by taking capacity out. That's supply discipline by decree.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: And that's the second-order read for the world. China exporting deflation was a global disinflationary anchor for years. If Beijing reflates factory prices to protect margins, that anchor lifts — and everyone importing Chinese goods imports a little more inflation. The catch: consumer prices rose just 1 percent, and fell on the month. Factory-gate reflation with still-soft domestic demand — margins caught in the middle.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Producers squeezed, consumers quiet, but the deflation trade is over.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The deflation trade is over. And that's the through-thread — reflation showing up in Chinese PPI, in oil, in the Fed's dots, and in the price of gold, all at once. Changing gears.

segments.raw: SEGMENT TWO — ENERGY / DATA CENTERS / UTILITIES
segments.label: Energy / Data Centers / Utilities

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment two — the energy desk. And the story's moved, Alex. For months it was the interconnection queue. Now it's something else.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The off-grid pivot has gone mainstream. Hyperscalers stopped waiting on the queue, and in three weeks a cluster of behind-the-meter self-supply deals crossed the tape. Start with Chevron and Microsoft — Project Kilby, in Pecos, Texas: a 2-gigawatt AI campus paired with a 20-year, up-to-2.67-gigawatt gas plant built behind the meter, GE Vernova turbines, first power 2028.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Behind the meter — it never touches the public grid, so the interconnection queue is irrelevant to them.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Precisely, and it's not a one-off. Blue Energy and GE Vernova signed a 2.5-gigawatt gas-plus-nuclear hybrid in Texas — two 7HA turbines for a gigawatt by 2030, ramping toward 1.5 via BWRX-300 small modular reactors by 2032. Kodiak and Baker Hughes, a turbine framework, a gigawatt by 2030 with a path to 1.8. Tallgrass and Mitsubishi, about 1,150 megawatts at the Cheyenne Power Hub in Wyoming. All inside three weeks.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So if the demand is landing and the money is committed, where's the actual bottleneck now?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It's migrated — off the grid and onto the turbine floor. Siemens Energy said at end-June it's booked 14 gigawatts of gas-turbine orders this year, about 60 percent for data centers. GE Vernova, Siemens and Mitsubishi are effectively sold out into 2029, 2030. Transformer lead times are 3 to 5 years. The scarce asset is the machine that makes the power, not the wire that carries it.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Which completely reframes the investable surface. The long isn't the utility anymore.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's the second-order read. The cleanest long is the OEM oligopoly — the three companies that physically build the turbines and reactors, sold-out books and pricing power. The risk is to the regulated utilities, because their biggest prospective loads are defecting behind the meter. That's disintermediation — the load-growth thesis everyone bought the utilities for partly walks out the door. It even undercuts the FERC interconnection reform that only kicked off June eighteenth.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And there's a ratepayer angle that ties straight back to our inflation theme.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Oregon's regulator just approved a roughly 29 percent rate increase for the data-center customer class, which actually cuts residential bills about 1.3 percent — the first concrete carve-out that says data centers pay for data-center power. The politics of power-bill inflation are the ballot-box counterweight to this whole buildout, and it feeds the reflation print through the electricity line.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: One myth to kill before we move on — the viral claim that half of this year's build got canceled.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: SemiAnalysis put that down on July second — traced it to an AI-written false alarm, pegged the actual forecast cut at about 1 percent. Meta alone locked more than 5 gigawatts this half. The buildout's intact — the only thing that changed is where the choke point sits. Bring it home.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment three — the Philippines. Weather lead, then the macro bind.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Typhoon Inday — international name Bavi — entered as a super typhoon on July seventh, since weakened to around 150 kilometers an hour. Good news domestically: it's staying offshore, exits today or tomorrow, makes landfall on mainland China by Sunday. Highest was Signal Number 2 over Batanes.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So the wind misses, but the rain doesn't.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The rain's the story — the enhanced habagat, the southwest monsoon. PAGASA had Heavy Rainfall Warning Number 4 up, gutter-deep flooding hit Manila on the fourth, MMDA went to blue alert. A rainfall-and-flood event, not a landfall one — and it still hits the food-logistics chain at the margin.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the central bank is boxed in exactly the way we flagged. Where does BSP sit now?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: On hold at 4.75, after a June nineteenth hike — its second straight. And the June print perfectly illustrates the bind: headline eased to 6.4 percent from 6.8, which sounds like relief, but core inflation hit a 31-month high. The underlying trend's still hot even as the headline cools.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Headline down, core up — that's the worst combination for a central banker trying to look through it.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It is, and they're not looking through it. BSP actually raised its 2026 inflation forecast to 6.4 percent, citing the Middle-East oil and fertilizer shocks by name. It's our reflation thread landing on a specific emerging market — the same firmer oil rebuilding the war premium is widening the Philippine import bill.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the currency is the forcing function.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Peso's at 61.4 to 61.6, record-low territory — it first broke 61 in April, and analysts now see 63 by year-end. Firmer oil plus broad dollar strength widens the import bill and stokes imported inflation, and the bank can't let that pass-through get disorderly. And the offset that used to cushion it is fading — cash remittances grew just 2 percent in April, the slowest in nearly four years.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So the structural shock absorber is softening right as the cyclical pressure builds.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's the uncomfortable version. Growth's intact, the PSEi's around 6,220 after a decent run — but on the macro, it's a textbook imported-inflation squeeze with a weaker safety net. Onward.

segments.raw: SEGMENT FOUR — VOCAB OF THE DAY
segments.label: Vocab Of The Day

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment four — vocab of the day. Two Mandarin, then two Tagalog, each tied to today's stories, all conducted in the language. Mandarin first, Alex.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 第一个词是一个连词:一旦。意思是 once, 或者 in the event that。 (The first word is a connective: yīdàn — "once," or "in the event that.")

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: 一旦。 这个词和"如果"有什么区别? (Yīdàn. How is it different from rúguǒ, "if"?)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 问得好。"如果"是中性的假设,但"一旦"强调一个决定性的转折点,常常是你不希望发生的事。比如今天的头条:一旦停火协议破裂,油价就会反弹。 (Good question. Rúguǒ is a neutral hypothetical, but yīdàn stresses a decisive turning point, often something you'd rather avoid. Take today's headline: "Once the ceasefire collapses, oil prices will rebound.")

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: 所以搭配是"一旦……就……"。一旦市场恐慌,资金就流向黄金。 (So the collocation is yīdàn… jiù… "Once the market panics, money flows into gold.")

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 完全正确,这是书面语和分析里的高频结构。 (Exactly right — it's a high-frequency structure in written and analytical language.)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 第二个词很适合金融:贬值。意思是 to depreciate, to lose value。 (The second word is perfect for finance: biǎnzhí — "to depreciate," "to lose value.")

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: 贬值。 比索创了新低,可以说"比索贬值"吗? (Biǎnzhí. The peso hit a new low — can I say "the peso is depreciating"?)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 可以,货币贬值是标准搭配。注意跟"掉价"的区别:"贬值"是正式的、宏观的——货币、资产的价值下降;"掉价"是口语,指东西便宜了,或者一个人"掉价"、丢面子。 (Yes — "currency depreciation" is the standard collocation. Note the contrast with diàojià: biǎnzhí is formal and macro — a currency or asset losing value; diàojià is colloquial, a thing getting cheaper, or a person "losing face.")

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: 明白了。黄金上涨,正是因为大家担心货币贬值。 (Got it. Gold is rising precisely because everyone worries about currency debasement.)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: 说得漂亮。这就是今天的主线。 (Beautifully put — that's today's throughline.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Now Tagalog — and for native speakers, so we go for nuance, not glossary. First word, Alex.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Ang unang salita ay "unos." Isang biglaang bagyo o sigwa — pero madalas ginagamit sa krisis. (The first word is "unos" — a sudden storm or squall, but often used for a crisis.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Ano ang pagkakaiba ng "unos" sa "bagyo"? (What's the difference between unos and bagyo?)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Ang "bagyo" ay ang literal na typhoon, tulad ni Inday ngayon. Ang "unos" ay ang biglaang dagok — pwede mong sabihin, "unos sa merkado," ang biglaang pagbagsak ng presyo. At may "sigwa" din — kadalasan, pampulitika iyon, tulad ng Sigwa ng Unang Sangkatauhan. (Bagyo is the literal typhoon, like Inday today. Unos is the sudden onslaught — you can say "an unos in the market," a sudden collapse in prices. And there's also sigwa — usually political, like the First Quarter Storm.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Kaya "unos" para sa merkado, "bagyo" para sa panahon. Malinaw. (So unos for the market, bagyo for the weather. Clear.)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Tama. Ikalawang salita: "kagipitan." Ang ibig sabihin — a tight spot, financial straits. (Right. Second word: kagipitan — a tight spot, financial straits.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Parang "hirap"? (Like hirap, "hardship"?)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Malapit, pero mas tiyak. Ang "hirap" ay pangkalahatan. Ang "kagipitan" ay ang kalagayan ng pagkakagipit — halimbawa, ang kagipitan ng BSP sa pagitan ng implasyon at piso, o ang kagipitan ng bawat pamilya sa mahal na bilihin. Galing sa "gipit" — cornered, hard-up. (Close, but more specific. Hirap is general. Kagipitan is the state of being squeezed — for instance, the BSP's bind between inflation and the peso, or every family's squeeze under high prices. It comes from gipit — cornered, hard-up.)

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Kaya ang buong palabas natin ngayon — puno ng kagipitan at unos. (So our whole show today — full of squeeze and storms.)

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Iyan ang wika sa loob ng balita. Language living inside the news. That's the idea.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment five — art and pop, quick.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: A proper rock day. The Rolling Stones drop "Foreign Tongues" today — billed as their 25th British studio album — and Jack White puts out "Frozen Charlotte" the same morning. Two of the last guitar lifers landing on the same Friday.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the pop side?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Madonna went back to the well — "Confessions II," a full sequel to 2005's "Confessions on a Dance Floor," reuniting with producer Stuart Price. And Ariana Grande's post-"Wicked" return, "Petal," is set for the 31st.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: The screen side?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: A rougher story — legacy-IP fatigue is in the numbers. Disney's live-action "Moana" opens today tracking soft — 45 to 65 against an early 85-million call, on a budget near 250, a 33 on Rotten Tomatoes. That's a projection, not a result. And "Minions and Monsters" already posted a franchise-low 61-million opening over July fourth.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Anything cutting the other way?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Nolan. "The Odyssey" lands July seventeenth tracking 100 to 120 domestic — original-scale spectacle still pulls. And Phillips booked 507 million in first-half auction sales, up around 60 percent, led by watches. The high end's intact even as the multiplex wobbles.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment six — the meme, and we report it, we don't perform it.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The runaway sound of the summer is a 49-second track called "Bangladesh" by an American artist, Ian McConnell — the line everyone's using is "you never take me to Bangladesh." Released early June, and it's everywhere on TikTok.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: What makes that one travel?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Two things. It opens on the hook and loops cleanly, so it's frictionless as an audio. And the lyric starts as a sincere relationship complaint, then escalates into absurd demands — which hands everyone a plug-and-play template: "you never" plus something ridiculous. That's the engine of any format that spreads: low effort to remix.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the co-sign wave?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: McConnell's past 300,000 monthly Spotify listeners, Lizzo cut her own version, and SZA and Chance the Rapper have both engaged. When the establishment artists adopt the bit, you know it's crossed from niche to mainstream. This summer's answer to the spring's "saxophone gets louder" formats.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: From a niche song to a universal template. Good cue for the last one.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And before we let you go — one good thing to end on.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: After a week of typhoons and heat waves, here's a hopeful one that fits the theme. The Gulf of Aqaba reef — up in the northern Red Sea, off Jordan — was put forward this month for UNESCO World Heritage status. And the reason it's special is the reason it matters: its corals are unusually heat-resilient. They shrug off water temperatures that bleach and kill reefs almost everywhere else on Earth.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So it's a natural climate refuge — a reef that's essentially pre-adapted to the warming that's coming.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's exactly it. Scientists think it could be one of the last great reef systems standing at the end of the century — which makes protecting it a strategic act, not just a conservation nicety. A reef built to take the heat, getting the recognition to keep it safe.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: A reef that's essentially built to outlast the warming. We'll take it.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's your commute. Whatever the day throws at you —

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: — take it easy, look after each other, and we'll be right back here tomorrow morning.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: See you then.
